name: destroy
context: Destroy Terraform-managed infrastructure safely with plan preview, targeted teardown, and lifecycle protection
info: The `terraform destroy` command tears down all infrastructure resources managed by the current Terraform configuration and state. It is the inverse of `terraform apply` — it reads the state, determines the destruction order based on the dependency graph, and removes resources in the correct sequence. Destroy respects the dependency graph: resources that depend on other resources are destroyed first, and their dependencies are destroyed after. Before running destroy, preview the exact impact with `terraform plan -destroy`. The `-auto-approve` flag skips confirmation for automated teardown in CI/CD. The `-target` flag restricts destruction to specific resources or modules. Resources can be protected from accidental destruction using the `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` meta-argument, which causes Terraform to error if a destroy is attempted on that resource. As an alternative to destroy, removing a resource block from configuration and running apply achieves selective removal. Destroy updates the state file as each resource is removed, so partial failures leave state reflecting reality. Always run `terraform plan -destroy` first in production.
triggers: prevent terraform from destroying a resource
body:
# terraform destroy

`terraform destroy` removes all resources defined in the current configuration and tracked in the state file. It is the clean teardown command for decommissioning infrastructure.

## Basic Usage

```bash
# Destroy with interactive confirmation
terraform destroy

# Destroy without confirmation (CI/CD)
terraform destroy -auto-approve

# Destroy in a different directory
terraform -chdir=environments/staging destroy

# Destroy with variables (if config requires them)
terraform destroy -var-file=staging.tfvars
```

## Always Preview First

In production, **always** preview destruction before executing:

```bash
# Preview what will be destroyed
terraform plan -destroy

# Save the destruction plan for review
terraform plan -destroy -out=destroy.tfplan

# View the saved plan
terraform show destroy.tfplan

# Apply the reviewed destruction plan
terraform apply destroy.tfplan
```

Using saved destruction plans is the safest approach — it guarantees the exact resources reviewed are the ones destroyed.

## Destroy Order and Dependency Graph

Terraform destroys resources in reverse dependency order:

```
Dependency:   VPC  -->  Subnet  -->  Security Group  -->  EC2 Instance
Destroy order: EC2 Instance  -->  Security Group  -->  Subnet  -->  VPC
```

```bash
# Visualize the dependency graph
terraform graph -type=destroy | dot -Tpng > destroy-graph.png

# Or view as text
terraform graph -type=destroy
```

Terraform resolves dependencies automatically. You do not need to specify destruction order — just run destroy and Terraform handles the sequence.

## Targeted Destruction

Destroy specific resources without affecting the rest:

```bash
# Destroy a single resource
terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.web

# Destroy an entire module
terraform destroy -target=module.database

# Destroy multiple specific resources
terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.web -target=aws_eip.web

# Preview targeted destruction first
terraform plan -destroy -target=aws_instance.web
```

Targeted destruction still respects the dependency graph — if the target has dependents, Terraform destroys them first.

## Protecting Resources with prevent_destroy

The `lifecycle` meta-argument `prevent_destroy` blocks destruction:

```hcl
resource "aws_rds_instance" "production" {
  identifier     = "prod-database"
  engine         = "postgres"
  instance_class = "db.r6g.xlarge"

  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" {
  bucket = "company-critical-data"

  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true
  }
}
```

With `prevent_destroy = true`, any plan that includes destroying this resource produces an error:

```
Error: Instance cannot be destroyed

  on main.tf line 1:
   1: resource "aws_rds_instance" "production" {

Resource aws_rds_instance.production has lifecycle.prevent_destroy
set, but the plan calls for this resource to be destroyed.
```

To destroy a protected resource, you must first remove `prevent_destroy` from the config and apply the change, then run destroy.

## Destroy vs Remove from Config

Two approaches to eliminating resources:

| Approach | How | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| `terraform destroy` | Run destroy command | Tearing down an entire environment |
| `terraform destroy -target` | Target specific resources | Removing specific resources from a live environment |
| Remove from `.tf` + `apply` | Delete the resource block, then apply | Selective removal as part of ongoing config changes |
| `terraform state rm` + delete | Remove from state, delete manually | Adopting resource into another config or manual management |

```bash
# Approach: Remove from config
# 1. Delete the resource block from your .tf files
# 2. Plan to see the destruction
terraform plan
# Output: "aws_instance.old will be destroyed"
# 3. Apply
terraform apply
```

Removing from config is preferred for selective changes because the destruction is part of your normal plan/apply/commit workflow.

## Handling Partial Destroy Failures

If destroy fails partway through:

```bash
# Check what remains in state
terraform state list

# Inspect specific resource
terraform state show aws_instance.web

# Retry the destroy
terraform destroy

# If a resource was deleted externally, refresh state
terraform apply -refresh-only

# If a resource is stuck, remove it from state
# (the real resource remains — delete it manually)
terraform state rm aws_instance.stuck
```

## CI/CD Teardown Pattern

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Destroy preview/staging environments after PR merge
ENV="${ENVIRONMENT:?Must set ENVIRONMENT}"
DIR="environments/$ENV"

export TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true
export TF_INPUT=0

# Init
terraform -chdir="$DIR" init -input=false

# Plan the destruction
terraform -chdir="$DIR" plan -destroy -out=destroy.tfplan -input=false -no-color

# Execute
terraform -chdir="$DIR" apply -no-color destroy.tfplan

echo "Environment $ENV destroyed successfully"
```

## Common Flags Reference

| Flag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `-auto-approve` | Skip interactive confirmation |
| `-target=ADDR` | Destroy only the specified resource/module |
| `-var="key=value"` | Set an input variable |
| `-var-file=FILE` | Load variables from a .tfvars file |
| `-parallelism=N` | Concurrent destroy operations (default 10) |
| `-input=false` | Disable interactive prompts |
| `-no-color` | Disable color output |
| `-refresh=false` | Skip state refresh before destroying |
| `-lock=false` | Disable state locking (not recommended) |
| `-lock-timeout=DURATION` | Time to wait for state lock |

sections.title: Preview destruction with plan -destroy
sections.body: Always run terraform plan -destroy before executing a destroy in production. This shows every resource that will be removed. Save the plan with -out for exact execution. The saved destruction plan can be reviewed by the team and then applied with terraform apply destroy.tfplan for auditable, predictable teardowns.

sections.title: Destroy order and dependency graph
sections.body: Terraform destroys resources in reverse dependency order — dependents are destroyed before their dependencies. An EC2 instance is destroyed before its subnet, which is destroyed before the VPC. This ordering is automatic based on the resource graph. Use terraform graph -type=destroy to visualize the order. Circular dependencies are detected at plan time.

sections.title: Targeted destruction
sections.body: Use -target to destroy specific resources or modules without affecting the rest of the infrastructure. Targeted destroy still respects the dependency graph — dependents of the target are also destroyed. Combine with plan -destroy -target for preview. Targeted operations should be followed by a full plan to verify consistency.

sections.title: Protecting resources with prevent_destroy
sections.body: The lifecycle meta-argument prevent_destroy = true causes Terraform to error if any plan includes destroying that resource. Use it for databases, storage buckets, and other critical resources that should never be accidentally deleted. To override, remove the setting from config and apply before destroying. This is a Terraform-level safety net, not a cloud-level protection.

sections.title: Destroy vs removing from config
sections.body: terraform destroy removes all or targeted resources. Removing a resource block from .tf files and running apply removes that specific resource as part of normal workflow. terraform state rm removes from state without deleting the real resource. Choose based on context: destroy for full teardown, config removal for selective changes, state rm for adoption into another config.

links.context: Parent CLI node with global flags and environment variables
Init must be run before destroy
Apply is the constructive counterpart to destroy

related.note: Destroy is the inverse of apply — it removes what apply created
Always plan -destroy before running destroy in production
Init must be run before destroy

scripts.name: Safe Destroy with Plan Preview
scripts.description: Preview destruction with plan -destroy, save the plan, and optionally apply it for safe teardown
scripts.inline:
set -euo pipefail
DIR="${TF_WORKING_DIR:-.}"
PLAN_FILE="${PLAN_OUT:-destroy.tfplan}"

export TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true
export TF_INPUT=0

# Build plan args
PLAN_ARGS="-destroy -input=false -out=$PLAN_FILE -no-color"

# Variable files
if [ -n "${VAR_FILE:-}" ]; then
  for vf in $(echo "$VAR_FILE" | tr ',' '\n'); do
    PLAN_ARGS="$PLAN_ARGS -var-file=$vf"
  done
fi

# Targets
if [ -n "${TARGET:-}" ]; then
  for t in $(echo "$TARGET" | tr ',' '\n'); do
    PLAN_ARGS="$PLAN_ARGS -target=$t"
  done
fi

# Plan the destruction
echo '=== Step 1: Plan Destruction ===' 
terraform -chdir="$DIR" plan $PLAN_ARGS

# Show summary
echo '' && echo '=== Destruction Summary ===' 
terraform -chdir="$DIR" show -json "$PLAN_FILE" | jq '{resources_to_destroy: [.resource_changes[]? | select(.change.actions[] == "delete") | .address]}'

# Apply if auto-execute is enabled
if [ "${AUTO_EXECUTE:-false}" = 'true' ]; then
  echo '' && echo '=== Step 2: Executing Destruction ==='
  terraform -chdir="$DIR" apply -no-color "$PLAN_FILE"
  echo '' && echo 'Destruction complete.'
  terraform -chdir="$DIR" state list 2>/dev/null | head -20 || echo '(state is empty)'
else
  echo '' && echo "Destruction plan saved to $PLAN_FILE"
  echo "Review and apply with: terraform -chdir=$DIR apply $PLAN_FILE"
  echo 'Or set AUTO_EXECUTE=true to destroy automatically.'
fi

TF_WORKING_DIR.description: Working directory with .tf files (default: current directory)

TARGET.description: Comma-separated resource addresses for targeted destruction

AUTO_EXECUTE.description: Set to 'true' to apply the destruction plan automatically

directory.description: Path to the Terraform working directory

remaining.description: Resource addresses still in state (empty on full success)

depends.reason: Init must be run before destroy

frontmatter.author: dojo-community
frontmatter.audience: developers, AI agents, DevOps engineers, SREs
